revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:神武景気]][[zh:神武景气]]


{{Referencesの明記|date=2020th year 4月}}
[[File:Amberley TV.jpg|thumb|300px|Television during the Jinmu Boom]]
'''Jinmu Keiki''' (神武景気) refers to a period of economic boom in {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}} from December {{ill|1954|en|1954|qid=Q18581|lt=1954|1=1954}} (Shōwa 29) to June {{ill|1957|en|1957|qid=Q5311|lt=1957|1=1957}} (Shōwa 32), marking the beginning of Japan's [[High Economic Growth]]. Until {{ill|1955|en|1955|qid=Q18577|lt=1955|1=1955}} (Shōwa 30), this boom was referred to as the '''Quantitative Boom''' (数量景気, Sūryō Keiki).

== Overview ==

The term "Jinmu Keiki" was coined in {{ill|1956|en|1956|qid=Q5221|lt=1956|1=1956}} (Shōwa 31), referring to an unprecedented economic boom since the ascension of Japan's legendary first emperor, {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|qid=Q200188|lt=Emperor Jimmu|1=Emperor Jimmu}}.

This economic boom was triggered by Japan's substantial involvement in supplying resources, repairing damaged tanks and aircraft, and supporting the {{ill|United States Armed Forces|en|United States Armed Forces|qid=Q11211|lt=United States Armed Forces|1=United States Armed Forces}} during the {{ill|Korean War|en|Korean War|qid=Q8663|lt=Korean War|1=Korean War}} (1950–1953). Known as the [[Korean War boom]] (朝鮮特需, Chōsen Tokujū), this led to significant expansion of the Japanese economy.

During this boom, Japan's economy surpassed its pre-war peak in {{ill|1940|en|1940|qid=Q18633|lt=1940|1=1940}} (Shōwa 15). The {{ill|1956|en|1956|qid=Q5221|lt=1956|1=1956}} Economic White Paper famously declared, "The post-war period is over," marking the completion. The economic boom also fueled the [[:en:consumer goods|consumer goods]] craze, popularizing the so-called [[Three Sacred Treasures (electrical appliances)|Three Sacred Treasures]] of modern households: the {{ill|refrigerator|en|refrigerator|qid=Q37828|lt=refrigerator|1=Refrigerator}}, {{ill|washing machine|en|washing machine|qid=Q124441|lt=washing machine|1=Washing machine}}, and [[:en:black-and-white television|black-and-white television]].

By the end of {{ill|1956|en|1956|qid=Q5221|lt=1956|1=1956}}, however, the boom receded significantly, earning the nickname "The Sunlit Boom" (天照らす景気, Amaterasu Keiki), reflecting its limited benefit to Japan's broader population. Despite the decline, the new nickname did not gain widespread use.

During this period, phrases like "The greatest since Emperor Jimmu" (神武以来, Jinmu Kono Kata) became popular to describe exceptional individuals or events (e.g., "The greatest prodigy since Jimmu" for {{ill|Hifumi Katō|en|Hifumi Katō|qid=Q11398957|lt=Hifumi Katō|1=Hifumi Katō}} or "The greatest beauty since Jimmu" for {{ill|Akihiro Miwa|en|Akihiro Miwa|qid=Q1076203|lt=Akihiro Miwa|1=Akihiro Miwa}}).

In {{ill|1957|en|1957|qid=Q5311|lt=1957|1=1957}} (Shōwa 32), the sharp economic activity led to a year-long [[Pot-bottom Recession]] (なべ底不況, Nabe Soko Fukyō). However, it was soon followed by the [[Iwato Keiki]], a 42-month-long period of economic prosperity.

== References ==

{{Reflist}}
